paper2.context: Matter exists in three states — solid, liquid and gas. Changes between states occur when energy is added or removed. Understanding particle theory helps explain the properties and behaviour of matter.

parts.question: Using the particle theory, describe the arrangement and movement of particles in (i) a solid, (ii) a liquid, and (iii) a gas.

rubric.value: solid: closely packed regular arrangement particles vibrate in fixed positions
liquid: closely packed but irregular particles can flow and slide past each other
gas: widely spread particles move rapidly in all directions fill any container

parts.question: Using diagrams or descriptions, name and describe FOUR changes of state.

rubric.value: melting solid to liquid energy absorbed
freezing liquid to solid energy released
evaporation or vaporisation liquid to gas energy absorbed
condensation gas to liquid energy released

parts.question: Distinguish between an element, a compound and a mixture. Give one example of each.

rubric.value: element: one type of atom only e.g. oxygen or iron
compound: two or more elements chemically combined e.g. water H2O
mixture: two or more substances not chemically combined e.g. air or salt water

paper2.context: Reproduction is essential for the continuation of species. Organisms reproduce either sexually or asexually. In flowering plants, sexual reproduction involves pollination, fertilisation and seed dispersal.

parts.question: Distinguish between sexual and asexual reproduction, giving ONE advantage and ONE example of each.

rubric.value: sexual requires two parents produces genetic variation
example of sexual: mammals flowering plants
asexual requires only one parent produces identical offspring quickly
example of asexual: budding in yeast binary fission in bacteria

parts.question: Describe the process of pollination and fertilisation in a flowering plant.

rubric.value: pollination: transfer of pollen from anther to stigma
pollen tube grows down style to ovary
fertilisation: male gamete fuses with female gamete (ovule) in ovary forming zygote

parts.question: Name and describe TWO methods of seed dispersal and explain their importance.

rubric.value: wind: light seeds e.g. dandelion carried by air currents
animal: seeds in fruits eaten or attached to fur spread to new locations

paper2.context: A balanced diet provides all nutrients needed for health in the correct proportions. Deficiencies in specific nutrients lead to characteristic diseases. The digestive system processes food for absorption.

parts.question: Name FOUR classes of nutrients and state ONE function of each.

rubric.value: carbohydrates: primary energy source for body functions
proteins: growth repair and maintenance of body tissues
fats: concentrated energy store and protection of organs
vitamins or minerals: regulation of body processes immune function bone health

parts.question: Describe the symptoms and cause of TWO nutritional deficiency diseases.

rubric.value: scurvy caused by lack of Vitamin C bleeding gums poor wound healing
rickets caused by lack of Vitamin D and calcium soft weak bones bowed legs

parts.question: Trace the path of a piece of bread through the digestive system, naming where digestion begins and ends and where absorption occurs.

rubric.value: digestion begins in the mouth with chewing and salivary amylase
further digestion in stomach and small intestine by enzymes
absorption of nutrients occurs in the small intestine

paper2.context: The transport system delivers essential substances to cells and removes waste. In humans, the circulatory system uses the heart, blood vessels and blood. In plants, xylem and phloem serve similar transport functions.

parts.question: State the functions of EACH component of human blood: red blood cells, white blood cells, platelets and plasma.

rubric.value: red blood cells: carry oxygen using haemoglobin
white blood cells: fight infection phagocytosis and antibody production
platelets: blood clotting to prevent blood loss
plasma: transports dissolved substances including nutrients hormones and waste

parts.question: Describe the journey of a red blood cell from the right atrium of the heart to the muscles in the leg and back to the heart.

rubric.value: right atrium to right ventricle
to lungs via pulmonary artery picks up oxygen becomes oxygenated
returns to left atrium left ventricle pumped out via aorta
travels through arteries to capillaries in leg muscles delivers oxygen
returns via veins to right atrium

parts.question: Distinguish between the functions of xylem and phloem in plants.

rubric.value: xylem transports water and dissolved minerals from roots upward to leaves
phloem transports dissolved sugars from leaves to all other parts of the plant

paper2.context: All living cells respire to obtain energy. Aerobic respiration uses oxygen and releases the maximum energy, while anaerobic respiration produces less energy without oxygen. Air pollution from combustion affects both human health and the environment.

parts.question: Write the word equation for aerobic respiration and state where in the cell it occurs.

rubric.value: glucose + oxygen → carbon dioxide + water + energy
occurs in the mitochondria

parts.question: Compare aerobic and anaerobic respiration in humans under THREE headings: oxygen requirement, products and energy released.

rubric.value: aerobic requires oxygen anaerobic does not
aerobic produces CO2 and water anaerobic produces lactic acid
aerobic releases much more energy anaerobic releases small amount

parts.question: Identify THREE air pollutants produced by burning fossil fuels and describe ONE harmful effect of each.

rubric.value: carbon dioxide: greenhouse effect and global warming
sulphur dioxide: acid rain damaging forests and aquatic life
carbon monoxide: combines with haemoglobin reducing oxygen transport causing poisoning

paper2.context: Excretion is the removal of metabolic waste products from the body. Multiple organs are involved. The kidneys are the main excretory organs, regulating water and waste removal through the process of urine formation.

parts.question: Define excretion and explain why it is important for maintaining homeostasis.

rubric.value: excretion is removal of metabolic waste products from the body
prevents toxic build-up of waste such as urea and CO2
maintains stable internal environment for cells to function properly

parts.question: State the role of EACH of the following in excretion: (i) kidneys (ii) lungs (iii) skin.

rubric.value: kidneys filter blood remove urea excess water and salts as urine
lungs excrete carbon dioxide produced by cellular respiration
skin excretes water salts and small amounts of urea through sweat

parts.question: Explain what is meant by osmoregulation and describe how the kidneys achieve it.

rubric.value: osmoregulation is control of water and salt balance in the blood
kidneys adjust amount of water reabsorbed in nephron to maintain blood concentration

paper2.topic: Sense Organs and the Nervous System
paper2.context: The sense organs detect stimuli from the environment and send information to the nervous system. The nervous system coordinates responses. Reflex actions provide rapid automatic protection.

parts.question: Describe the structure and function of the human eye, naming FOUR key parts.

rubric.value: cornea refracts light into the eye
iris controls pupil size regulating amount of light entering
lens focuses light onto retina by changing shape
retina contains receptor cells that convert light to nerve impulses sent to brain

parts.question: Draw and label a reflex arc. Describe the pathway of a reflex action using the example of withdrawing a hand from a hot object.

rubric.value: receptor in skin detects heat stimulus
sensory neurone carries impulse to spinal cord
relay neurone in spinal cord connects to motor neurone
motor neurone carries impulse to effector muscle contracts withdrawing hand

parts.question: Explain why reflex actions are important for survival.

rubric.value: they are fast and automatic not requiring conscious thought
they protect the body from harm before the brain has time to process the threat

paper2.context: Health encompasses physical, mental and social well-being. Diseases can be communicable (infectious) or non-communicable. Understanding how diseases spread and how they can be prevented is a core aspect of integrated science.

parts.question: Distinguish between communicable and non-communicable diseases. Give TWO examples of each.

rubric.value: communicable: spread from person to person e.g. tuberculosis cholera dengue malaria influenza
non-communicable: not spread between people e.g. diabetes hypertension cancer asthma

parts.question: Describe how malaria is transmitted and state THREE methods of controlling its spread.

rubric.value: transmitted by bite of infected female Anopheles mosquito
control: use of mosquito nets treated with insecticide
draining stagnant water to eliminate breeding sites
use of antimalarial drugs or indoor spraying

parts.question: Explain how vaccines protect against infectious diseases.

rubric.value: vaccine contains weakened dead pathogens or antigens
stimulates immune system to produce antibodies
memory cells remain so future infection triggers faster stronger response

parts.question: State THREE ways in which a healthy lifestyle can reduce the risk of non-communicable diseases.

rubric.value: regular physical exercise reduces risk of cardiovascular disease and obesity
balanced diet low in saturated fats and sugar
avoiding smoking and excessive alcohol consumption

paper2.context: Energy exists in many forms and can be transformed but not created or destroyed. Understanding electrical circuits and energy efficiency is essential for sustainable energy use.

parts.question: State the law of conservation of energy and give TWO examples of energy transformations.

rubric.value: energy cannot be created or destroyed only transformed from one form to another
chemical to kinetic: car engine burning fuel
electrical to light and heat: light bulb

parts.question: A circuit contains a 12V battery and a resistor of 6Ω. (i) Calculate the current. (ii) Calculate the power dissipated.
Explain the difference between a series circuit and a parallel circuit, giving ONE advantage of each.

rubric.value: series: one path for current all components share voltage
advantage of series: simple to construct
parallel: multiple paths each component gets full voltage
advantage of parallel: components work independently failure of one does not stop others

parts.question: Calculate the efficiency of a motor that receives 500 J of electrical energy and produces 350 J of useful mechanical energy.

rubric.value: efficiency = useful output / total input × 100
efficiency = 70%

paper2.context: Metals and non-metals have characteristic properties and reactions. Understanding acids, bases and the pH scale is fundamental to chemistry. Various laboratory techniques are used to separate mixtures.

parts.question: Compare the physical properties of metals and non-metals under FOUR headings.

rubric.value: conductivity: metals conduct heat and electricity non-metals generally do not
lustre: metals shiny non-metals dull
malleability: metals can be beaten into sheets non-metals brittle
state at room temperature: most metals solid non-metals may be solid liquid or gas

parts.question: Explain what happens during a neutralisation reaction. Write a word equation and give ONE everyday application.

rubric.value: acid reacts with base to form salt and water
acid + base → salt + water
application e.g. treating acid indigestion with antacid treating acid soils with lime

parts.question: Describe how you would use filtration and evaporation to obtain pure salt from a mixture of salt and sand.

rubric.value: dissolve mixture in water sand is insoluble
filter to remove sand residue
collect filtrate containing dissolved salt
evaporate water from filtrate to obtain salt crystals

parts.question: Explain why iron is more reactive than copper, using the reactivity series.

rubric.value: the reactivity series ranks metals by tendency to lose electrons and react
iron is higher than copper in the reactivity series
so iron can displace copper from its salt solutions and reacts more readily with oxygen and water

paper2.context: Ecosystems are complex communities of organisms interacting with each other and their environment. Human activity increasingly threatens natural ecosystems through pollution, deforestation and climate change. Alternative energy sources offer solutions to reduce environmental impact.

parts.question: Define an ecosystem and describe the roles of producers, consumers and decomposers within it.

rubric.value: ecosystem is community of living organisms interacting with abiotic environment
producers make own food by photosynthesis form base of food chains
consumers eat other organisms primary consumers eat plants secondary eat animals
decomposers break down dead matter recycling nutrients back to soil

parts.question: Explain the carbon cycle, describing how carbon enters and leaves the atmosphere.

rubric.value: carbon enters atmosphere as CO2 from respiration and combustion of fossil fuels
carbon removed from atmosphere by photosynthesis in plants

parts.question: Identify THREE threats to aquatic ecosystems in the Caribbean and suggest ONE solution for each.

rubric.value: coral reef destruction from pollution and tourism — regulate tourist activity and reduce runoff
eutrophication from agricultural runoff — reduce fertiliser use buffer zones near water
oil spills from shipping — stricter regulations and rapid response plans

paper2.context: Forces govern all motion and can be used through simple machines to do useful work. As fossil fuels are depleted and their environmental costs become clearer, alternative energy sources become increasingly important especially for small island Caribbean states.

parts.question: State Newton's Three Laws of Motion, giving a sporting or everyday example for each.

rubric.value: First Law inertia: object stays at rest or uniform motion unless net force acts e.g. passenger lurches forward when car brakes
Second Law F=ma: greater force produces greater acceleration e.g. pushing harder makes a ball roll faster
Third Law: equal opposite reaction e.g. rocket propulsion gun recoil swimmer pushing wall

parts.question: Explain how a Class 2 lever (e.g. a wheelbarrow) works, identifying the positions of fulcrum, load and effort and calculating the mechanical advantage if the load is 600N and the effort is 150N.

rubric.value: fulcrum at one end load between fulcrum and effort
mechanical advantage = load/effort = 600/150 = 4

parts.question: Compare solar energy and wind energy as alternative energy sources for the Caribbean, giving ONE advantage and ONE disadvantage of each.

rubric.value: solar advantage: abundant sunshine in Caribbean low running costs
solar disadvantage: intermittent depends on sunlight storage needed
wind advantage: clean renewable no fuel costs
wind disadvantage: intermittent only where wind is reliable visual and noise impact
both suit Caribbean context reducing dependence on imported fossil fuels
